did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes BLSNet, a Broad Learning System (BLS) using an incremental learning algorithm, for the detection and classification of non-melanoma and melanoma skin lesions from dermoscopic images. The study evaluates this AI model on the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets, achieving an accuracy of 99.09% and an F1-score of 98.73%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel algorithm (BLSNet) for skin lesion detection. It includes original sections such as 'Material and method', 'Results' (reporting specific accuracy and execution time metrics on ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets), and 'Conclusion', indicating it reports new experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: BLSNet: Skin lesion detection and classification using broad learning system with incremental learning algorithm
authors: V. S. S. P. Raju Gottumukkala
brief_summary: This study proposes BLSNet, a Broad Learning System utilizing an incremental learning algorithm, for the detection and classification of non-melanoma and melanoma skin lesions from dermoscopic images. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets to address the high computational costs and retraining requirements associated with traditional deep learning architectures. Experimental results demonstrated that BLSNet outperformed existing deep learning-based models, achieving an accuracy of 99.09% and an F1-score of 98.73%. Additionally, the proposed method achieved a superior overall execution time of 0.93 seconds. The study concludes that BLSNet effectively balances classification accuracy with execution time, offering a fast and accurate alternative for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is the classification of 'non-melanoma and melanoma skin lesions'. Melanoma is identified as a high-mortality skin cancer and the primary malignant target for detection and classification in the experiments on ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets.

The study classifies 'non-melanoma' lesions alongside melanoma. The text mentions differentiating melanomas from 'benign lesions' and classifying lesions as 'benign or malignant'. While specific non-melanoma subtypes (like BCC, SCC, AK) are mentioned in the context of dataset challenges (e.g., HAM10000), the experimental task is framed as binary or broad non-melanoma vs. melanoma classification.

Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the skin lesion forms in the HAM10000 dataset where image elements are comparable to melanoma and AKIEC, making classification difficult. Not explicitly listed as a primary target class for the BLSNet model's final output in the abstract/results, which focus on melanoma vs. non-melanoma.

Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the skin lesion forms in the HAM10000 dataset with comparable image elements to melanoma and BCC. Not explicitly listed as a primary target class for the BLSNet model's final output.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a condition that can progress from melanoma or be confused with it ('atypical nevus that is progressed from melanoma'). It is part of the broader 'benign' category but not explicitly detailed as a separate experimental class label in the results.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is the 'classification of non-melanoma and melanoma skin lesions from dermoscopic images'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma or non-melanoma/benign). The output is a final classification result derived from averaging multiple sub-classification outcomes using a sigmoid activation function. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The publicly available and real-time ISIC 2019 challenge dataset, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the proposed BLSNet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed BLSNet model.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 200 skin lesion images with a maximum resolution of 765 x 574 pixels, obtained from the Hospital Pedro Hispano's Dermatology Service.

use.training: Used for training the proposed BLSNet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed BLSNet model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC-2019.notes: The abstract reports an accuracy of 99.09% and F1-score of 98.73% for the proposed model on ISIC 2019, while Table 2 reports 99.01% accuracy and 99.12% F1-score. The paper states BLSNet outperforms existing DL-based models.

PH2.notes: The PH2 dataset includes 200 skin lesion images with classes: benign skin cancer, malignant skin cancer, and melanoma skin cancer.

architecture_used.main_model: BLSNet (Broad Learning System with Incremental Learning Algorithm)

architecture_details.structure: Flat network form without deep structure or stacked layers
architecture_details.mechanism: Real inputs are transferred and placed as mapped features in feature nodes. The architecture is elaborated in broad sense in the enhancement nodes. Incremental learning algorithms are implemented for fast remodelling in wide expansion without retraining.
training_stages: Random weight generation for both mapping & enhancing features
Computing the weight coefficients difference among the hidden and output layers
architecture_details.activation_function: Sigmoid activation function used in enhancement nodes
architecture_details.classification_method: Average of multiple sub BLS-ILA classification outcomes

training_details.sampling_method: Bootstrap sampling used to optimize training and testing problems and generate multiple sub-training datasets
training_details.retraining: Avoided; network expands gradually when new nodes are required and input data is continually entering

classification_or_output_head.details: Final classification result (cp) is calculated by averaging the predicted values (cpi) of multiple sub BLS-ILA classification outcomes using a sigmoid activation function.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to optimize training and testing problems generated in the dataset. It generates multiple sub-training datasets to handle additional input data and feature size/property mismatches without retraining the whole model. Applied to important features (ImpILA_BLS) to reduce classification errors.

conclusions: The proposed BLSNet model achieves a performance trade-off between classification accuracy and execution time for skin lesion detection and classification.
BLSNet outperforms existing deep learning-based skin lesion detection and classification models, achieving an accuracy of 99.09% and an F1-score of 98.73%.
The proposed BLSNet model demonstrates superior execution time (0.93 seconds) compared to conventional deep learning approaches.
The use of an incremental learning algorithm allows the network to extend neural nodes and update weights gradually without requiring complete retraining when new data is introduced.
Bootstrap sampling is utilized to optimize training and testing problems, addressing feature size and property mismatches caused by the continuous addition of new images to skin cancer datasets.
important_remarks: The paper reports conflicting accuracy and F1-score values for the ISIC 2019 dataset between the abstract (99.09% accuracy, 98.73% F1) and the results table (99.01% accuracy, 99.12% F1), indicating a lack of internal consistency in reporting.
The study relies on the PH2 dataset, which contains only 200 images, raising concerns about the generalizability and robustness of the model due to the small sample size.

The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split sizes for the ISIC 2019 or PH2 datasets, nor does it provide confidence intervals or statistical significance tests for the performance metrics.
The model is evaluated on only two datasets (ISIC 2019 and PH2); the lack of external validation on diverse, independent clinical datasets limits the assessment of real-world clinical applicability.

The paper acknowledges that differentiating melanomas from benign lesions is difficult due to artifacts, noises, and comparable image elements (e.g., BCC vs. melanoma in HAM10000), but does not provide a detailed analysis of error cases or confusion matrices.

The claim of 'superior' performance is based on comparisons with cited literature rather than direct, side-by-side re-evaluation of baseline models on the same data splits, which may introduce comparison bias.

The paper mentions using bootstrap sampling to optimize training but does not explicitly detail how data leakage was prevented or if the bootstrap samples were strictly derived from the training set only.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma/benign) rather than multi-class classification of specific non-melanoma subtypes (e.g., BCC, SCC, AK), limiting its granularity for clinical decision-making.
